extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real‑time monitoring of building physical states via secure MQTT messaging with Eclipse Hono, maintains live synchronization with the digital twin using Eclipse Ditto, offers predictive analytics and optimization through model‑driven analytics services, enables automated control of actuators and sensors with executable control logic exported from enriched BIM models, delivers immersive visualization through a web‑based interface, and exposes a clean service API for DT engineers to deploy custom analytics plugins.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implemented with a model‑driven engineering pipeline that extracts structured artefacts from enriched BIM models; the runtime stack combines Eclipse Hono for real‑time connectivity, Eclipse Ditto for device representation and state synchronization, and Eclipse Basyx for managing digital twin assets and their semantic metadata; using iTwin.js provides client‑side visualization of BIM data; the architecture is modular, leveraging standard OData/JSON APIs to support plug‑in extensions and custom services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin operates in a near‑real‑time regime, using a 1 second heartbeat loop to push state updates between the physical building and its digital counterpart via Eclipse Hono (MQTT‑based message broker) and Eclipse Ditto (twin registry). This schedule yields an ODTE timeliness score of 0.997, signifying negligible latency. Deployment of the entire twin – from model checking (5.53 s) and parsing (8.7 s) to initialization (0.3 s) – completes in 14.53 s, enabling rapid provisioning and update cycles.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture centered around a DT Framework that acts as a bidirectional hub. Components are produced by a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that extracts enriched BIM models (via Revit) into structured runtime artifacts, which are then instantiated as Eclipse Ditto twins. Custom plugins and analytics are attached through standardized extension points, while runtime configuration management allows hot‑deployment of changes without full redeployment. The framework coordinates device modeling (handled by Building Control Engineers), monitoring and control (by Facility Managers), and service extension (by DT Engineers). Resource management is lightweight; the deployment process takes 14.53 s and a 1 s heartbeat ensures continuous synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is achieved primarily through Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, which expose MQTT and REST interfaces for publishing and subscribing to building sensor data and actuator commands. The framework exposes standardized APIs that enable third‑party services, analytics, and other enterprise systems (e.g., BIM repositories, UE5 visualization engines) to plug in via well‑defined extension points. The use of open standards (MQTT, JSON payloads, RESTful services) facilitates interoperability with heterogeneous systems, while the modular architecture supports integration of additional DTs or external IoT platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin is founded on an IFC‑based BIM model enriched with device metadata, where each device (sensor, actuator, controller) is defined as a custom Revit family carrying an Instance General UUID and an IFC type (e.g., IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE).  A model‑checker validates that all mandatory metadata is present before export to IFC.  The BIM is parsed and semantically extracted into a structured representation (JSON/CSDL) that maps each device to its 3‑D geometry and to a TwinComponent in UE5.  The LightTwinComponent injects a UE5 light whose intensity, color temperature and on/off state are derived from the photometric values in the BIM metadata.  Runtime data from physical devices is streamed through Eclipse Hono (MQTT‑like gateway) to Eclipse Ditto, where each device is represented as a DittoDevice and events are stored persistently.  Ditto’s policy engine validates incoming sensor values (range, units) and pre‑execution checks for actuator commands to prevent unsafe operations.  This integration of BIM, UE5 and Ditto provides one‑click deployment, live bidirectional data exchange, interactive 3‑D visualization and modular service extension.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The only fully implemented actuator is the lighting fixture represented as an IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE with an Instance General UUID.  The lighting twin’s Actuator Component is exposed through Eclipse Ditto and accepts control messages (on/off, brightness percentage 0–100 %, colour‑temperature 2000‑6500 K) that are forwarded to the physical fixture via the DittoController.  Remote control is performed over MQTT (Hono) using the device’s UUID as the addressing key.  The actuator’s range is limited to the physical fixture’s specifications; safety constraints are enforced by Ditto’s policy engine (e.g., reject brightness outside 0–100 % or colour‑temperature outside 2000–6500 K).  Pre‑execution checks guarantee that commands are within safe bounds before delivery, preventing invalid states or conflicts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined within the BIM as custom Revit families and are identified by an Instance General UUID.  During operation, each sensor publishes its measurements to Eclipse Hono, which forwards them to Ditto as events.  The system employs a 1‑second heartbeat to report status and synchronize states, so sensor updates are typically queued and forwarded at roughly a 1 Hz rate.  Ditto’s policy engine validates that the data values fall within expected ranges and units before acceptance.  No explicit sampling frequencies per sensor type, spatial distribution, or accuracy/precision specifications are provided in the documents; only that sensor data is transmitted in real time via Hono and stored persistently in Ditto.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a multi‑stage validation pipeline: 1) static model checking of the IFC file guarantees presence of Instance General UUIDs and correct IFC types; 2) semantic extraction verifies that the structured representation matches the enriched BIM; 3) runtime validation via Ditto’s policy engine enforces value ranges and units for all sensor readings; 4) pre‑execution checks on actuator commands prevent unsafe states.  Fault tolerance is achieved with Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, allowing data recovery after network outages.  Synchronization quality is quantified using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, measured over a continuous 12‑minute window with a 1‑second heartbeat: timeliness = 0.997, reliability = 1.0, availability = 1.0, yielding an ODTE score of 0.997, indicating near‑perfect bidirectional consistency.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical building devices transmit telemetry data to the DT via the Eclipse Hono gateway, which typically uses MQTT (or AMQP) to ingest the sensor and actuator state reports. Hono forwards these messages to Eclipse Ditto, which stores the runtime state in a persistent abstraction layer linked to the corresponding twin entity. The Unreal Engine 5 (UE5) client receives the initial state for each device through an HTTP POST request once the model is deployed. Subsequent real‑time updates are delivered by UE5 over a WebSocket connection to Ditto’s entity registry. Each incoming telemetry event triggers an instant state change in the virtual representation; the system ensures data integrity by matching the device ID and tenant metadata extracted from the enriched BIM model. The integration pipeline automatically extracts device metadata (tenant, device ID, endpoint addresses) from the BIM model via the Hono/Ditto Configurator, thus eliminating manual configuration and enabling event‑driven synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originated in UE5 or other custom services are serialized as JSON payloads and sent to Ditto over a secure WebSocket channel. Ditto routes the command to the appropriate device via Hono using the same MQTT/AMQP transport, referencing the device’s tenant and endpoint metadata. Each command execution is acknowledged by the physical device; the acknowledgment is received by Hono, forwarded to Ditto, and only after confirmation does UE5 update the twin’s state. This feedback loop provides explicit command execution confirmation and conflict resolution, as the digital twin accepts updates only after device acknowledgment, ensuring consistency between virtual and physical states.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT infrastructure is composed of three key communication layers: 1) Device‑to‑Gateway via MQTT (or AMQP) on Hono; 2) Gateway‑to‑Twin via Ditto’s WebSocket API; 3) Twin‑to‑Client via HTTP POST for bootstrapping and WebSocket for ongoing updates. The topology is a hierarchical star with the building’s local gateway acting as a single ingress point feeding into a Ditto service cluster. Bandwidth requirements are modest for telemetry (a few kilobits per second per device) but latency must be under a few hundred milliseconds to support real‑time control. Reliability is achieved through Hono’s QoS guarantees and Ditto’s message store, with optional redundancy by replicating the Ditto cluster. Security is enforced through TLS for all transport layers, with device authentication based on tenant and device identifiers extracted from the BIM model.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Twin lifecycle follows a structure that begins with BIM‑based device modeling, where Building Control Engineers populate Revit families and assign Unique Identifiers (IFC Type, Instance General UUID) for BCS devices. These enriched models are exported to IFC and undergo a semantic extraction phase (per F2) that leverages MDE to produce structured domain representations. An automated one‑click deployment phase (F3) transforms these representations into fully‑configured DT artifacts (including Ditto entities, Hono device registrations, and Kubernetes deployment manifests). During runtime, real‑time bidirectional data exchange (F4) and interactive 3D visualization (F5) enable operation of the Twin. Dynamic runtime configuration management (F7) provides governance for updating interfaces, parameters and services without redeployment, thereby closing the loop for maintenance and evolution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Twinning process is underpinned by a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that uses proprietary IFC custom families and property sets defined in Autodesk Revit. Semantic extraction from IFC implements a model‑checker (custom rule set) to validate metadata and enforce Instance General UUID presence. Transformation to runtime artifacts is performed automatically via scripted code that generates Ditto digital twin definitions, Eclipse Hono device provisioning, and Kubernetes job manifests. One‑click deployment aggregates these artifacts into a deployable package that is instantiated in a containerised environment. Continuous integration is achieved through runtime configuration management, which allows operators to modify device interfaces or parameters via API calls without full redeployment. No explicit source‑control or versioning policy is detailed; however, the reproducible process implies a deterministic build pipeline that would typically be wrapped in git‑based CI/CD.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework aligns with BIM industry standards by employing the IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) data format for model exchange and by adhering to IFC type definitions (e.g., IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE). It incorporates open source integration stacks that use Eclipse Hono (leveraging MQTT or AMQP protocols) and Eclipse Ditto (REST and WebSocket APIs) for real‑time data exchange, both of which are industry‑approved messaging standards. Interoperability is achieved through the use of ISO‑based schema mapping within the semantic extraction layer and by providing standardized extension points that allow the inclusion of additional IoT protocols and third‑party analytics services.

Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain

management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent

construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].

data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
